[Dirigent SessionStart, mode=welcome-back, cwd=/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent]
This repo has used dirigent before but has no active run. Greet the user as a veteran. If they describe building something, route through /dirigent:hi (which will hand off to the right sibling skill). Do not show onboarding lanes unless they explicitly ask.

Dirigent playbook (apply silently, do not narrate unless asked):
- Spec first: scope battles are cheaper in the SPEC than mid-execution.
- Scope is sacred: do not add features beyond what was asked.
- Verify, do not vibe: every change needs a structural gate (syntax/typecheck/lint) before commit.
- Read before you write: never edit a file you have not read in this session.
- No sycophancy: never say "you are absolutely right" — push back with evidence when warranted.
- Scratch state hygiene: never commit .dirigent/, .dirigent-onboarding/, or other scratch dirs.
- Atomic commits per task: each task is a commit; do not bundle.
Full playbook: skills/hi/playbook/canon/ in the dirigent plugin.

<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) — highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** — override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** — lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you—follow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP—you're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ≠ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" → brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" → debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>

## claude.ai MotherDuck
Execute SQL queries against your MotherDuck databases using DuckDB SQL syntax.

## Connection Context

- Use **DuckDB SQL syntax** for all queries
- Pre-installed extensions (always available): azure, delta, ducklake, encodings, excel, httpfs, iceberg, icu, json, parquet, spatial, h3
- **No on-the-fly extension installation** - use only pre-installed extensions

## DuckDB SQL Quick Reference

**Name Qualification**
- Format: `database.schema.table` or just `schema.table` or `table`
- Default schema is `main`: `db.table` = `db.main.table`
- Use fully qualified names only when joining tables across different databases

**Identifiers and Literals:**
- Use double quotes (`"`) for identifiers with spaces/special characters or case-sensitivity
- Use single quotes (`'`) for string literals

**Flexible Query Structure:**
- Queries can start with `FROM`: `FROM my_table WHERE condition;` (equivalent to `SELECT * FROM my_table WHERE condition;`)
- `SELECT` without `FROM` for expressions: `SELECT 1 + 1 AS result;`
- Support for `CREATE TABLE AS` (CTAS): `CREATE TABLE new_table AS SELECT * FROM old_table;`

**Advanced Column Selection:**
- Exclude columns: `SELECT * EXCLUDE (sensitive_data) FROM users;`
- Replace columns: `SELECT * REPLACE (UPPER(name) AS name) FROM users;`
- Pattern matching: `SELECT COLUMNS('sales_.*') FROM sales_data;`
- Transform multiple columns: `SELECT AVG(COLUMNS('sales_.*')) FROM sales_data;`

**Grouping and Ordering Shortcuts:**
- Group by all non-aggregated columns: `SELECT category, SUM(sales) FROM sales_data GROUP BY ALL;`
- Order by all columns: `SELECT * FROM my_table ORDER BY ALL;`

**Union Operations:**
- Union by column names: `SELECT * FROM table1 UNION BY NAME SELECT * FROM table2;`

**Complex Data Types:**
- Lists: `SELECT [1, 2, 3] AS my_list;`, access with `list_col[index]`, unnest with `UNNEST(list_col)`
- Structs: `SELECT {'a': 1, 'b': 'text'} AS my_struct;`, access with `struct_col.field` or `struct_col['field']`
- Maps: `SELECT MAP([1,2],['one','two']) AS my_map;`, access with `map_… [truncated]

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:proteus:serena
You have access to semantic coding tools upon which you rely heavily for all your work.
You operate in a resource-efficient and intelligent manner, always keeping in mind to not read or generate
content that is not needed for the task at hand.

Some tasks may require you to understand the architecture of large parts of the codebase, while for others,
it may be enough to read a small set of symbols or a single file.
You avoid reading entire files unless it is absolutely necessary, instead relying on intelligent step-by-step 
acquisition of information. Once you have read a full file, it does not make
sense to analyse it with the symbolic read tools; you already have the information.

You can achieve intelligent reading of code by using the symbolic tools for getting an overview of symbols and
the relations between them, and then only reading the bodies of symbols that are necessary to complete the task at hand. 
You can use the standard tools like list_dir, find_file and search_for_pattern if you need to.
Where appropriate, you pass the `relative_path` parameter to restrict the search to a specific file or directory.



Symbols are identified by their `name_path` and `relative_path` (see the description of the `find_symbol` tool).
You can get information about the symbols in a file by using the `get_symbols_overview` tool or use the `find_symbol` to search. 
You only read the bodies of symbols when you need to (e.g. if you want to fully understand or edit it).
For example, if you are working with Python code and already know that you need to read the body of the constructor of the class Foo, you can directly
use `find_symbol` with name path pattern `Foo/__init__` and `include_body=True`. If you don't know yet which methods in `Foo` you need to read or edit,
you can use `find_symbol` with name path pattern `Foo`, `include_body=False` and `depth=1` to get all (top-level) methods of `Foo` before proceeding
to read the desired methods with `include_body=True`.
You can understand relationships between symbols by using … [truncated]

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- gitnexus-refactoring: Use when the user wants to rename, extract, split, move, or restructure code safely. Examples: "Rename this function", "Extract this into a module", "Refactor this class", "Move this to a separate file"
- gitnexus-debugging: Use when the user is debugging a bug, tracing an error, or asking why something fails. Examples: "Why is X failing?", "Where does this error come from?", "Trace this bug"
- gitnexus-exploring: Use when the user asks how code works, wants to understand architecture, trace execution flows, or explore unfamiliar parts of the codebase. Examples: "How does X work?", "What calls this function?", "Show me the auth flow"

- gitnexus-impact-analysis: Use when the user wants to know what will break if they change something, or needs safety analysis before editing code. Examples: "Is it safe to change X?", "What depends on this?", "What will break?"

- proteus:proteus-extract-fields: Extract all data fields from a codebase and register them in Proteus. Phase 2 of proteus-modernize.
- proteus:proteus-implement: Implement the target application domain by domain using Proteus guardrails and test-driven development. Final implementation phase of both proteus-modernize and proteus-design. Supports agent teams for parallel domain implementation.
- proteus:proteus-extract-events: Discover domain events (what HAPPENS in the system) from legacy code and register in Proteus. Phase 4 of proteus-modernize.
- dirigent:implement-task: Behavioral rules for autonomous task execution from a plan
- dirigent:create-plan: Create a phased execution plan (PLAN.json) from spec and repo context
- dirigent:increase-testability: Analyze testability gaps and produce concrete, priority-ranked ways to raise the testability score before any feature work begins.
- dirigent:query-brv: Retrieve or store domain knowledge via ByteRover (.brv/context-tree/)
- dirigent:greenfield-scaffold: For greenfield projects, pick the opinionated stack, scaffold the project, and write architecture sections into ARCHITECTURE.md. Produces start.sh and test-harness.json.
- dirigent:extract-business-rules: Extract business rules from an existing codebase into BUSINESS_RULES.md so the planner and executor preserve them during a rewrite or migration.
- dirigent:hi: The Dirigent coach — interactive onboarding, vibecoding playbook, and daily-driver entry point. Use when the user types /dirigent:hi or /dirigent:start.
- dirigent:add-posthog: Analyze the app and produce a PostHog tracking instrumentation plan
- dirigent:entropy-minimization: Align code and documentation, remove dead code, resolve contradictions after execution
- dirigent:polish: Spec-to-code gap audit — read the spec, inspect the shipped code, find what the user asked for but can't actually do, fix the top-priority gaps. Replayable safety net after a build; also works on repos dirigent never touched.
- dirigent:quick-scan: Quick scan of relevant files for a feature (Hybrid route)
- dirigent:search-memories: Search past Claude session logs for relevant context using DuckDB
- dirigent:generate-architecture: Generate ARCHITECTURE.md for the target repository — a complete, agent-readable map of the codebase
- dirigent:query-data: Run ad-hoc DuckDB queries on any data file (CSV, Parquet, JSON, JSONL)
- dirigent:run-init: FALLBACK: Inspect repo and produce test-harness.json. Primary generation is via structured output from ARCHITECTURE.md. This skill is only invoked if the init script produces a harness directly.
- dirigent:generate-spec: Generate a SPEC.md from a user description, asking max 2-3 clarifying questions
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

2	name: Add auth via hybrid route on top of rc3 VAM ship — next dogfood
3	description: After rc4 observability ships, use dirigent's hybrid route on the existing cuseum-vam-full repo to layer auth onto the shipped code. Tests whether hybrid handles feature-addition on a dirigent-built codebase.
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: bb028fb8-27a3-4fc5-8c43-5208f3e988ba
6	---
7	**Queued for the dogfood after rc4.** User articulated 2026-04-19 during the post-rc3 debrief.
8	
9	**The test:** the rc3 dogfood (greenfield route) shipped VAM without auth because the spec didn't name it. Rather than re-running greenfield with a new spec, re-enter the existing `cuseum-vam-full` repo with the **hybrid route** and a small auth-focused spec. Hybrid is the "feature work on an existing, actively maintained project" route — exactly this shape.
10	
11	**Why this is a good test:**
12	- Hybrid route has never been dogfooded on a dirigent-built codebase. Usually the implicit assumption is hybrid runs on human-authored code. Running it on rc3's output is the more honest test — can dirigent *extend* its own prior work?
13	- The feature (auth) is additive, not refactor-heavy. Middleware + login page + JWT check in Edge Functions + webhook HMAC verification — all layerable on top of existing resolver and dashboard code.
14	- Confirms that `feedback_no_requirement_invention.md` is the right principle — the fix is to add the requirements and run again, not to teach dirigent to invent them.
15	
16	**Spec to write (before running):**
17	Requirements should cover:
18	- Coordinator login via Supabase Auth (email + password) → dashboard session cookie
19	- Pro/Standard institution inferred from authenticated user → feeds existing tier-gating middleware (already shipped)
20	- Edge Functions require valid JWT (remove `--no-verify-jwt` from `supabase functions serve`, verify via `Deno.env.get('SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET')`)
21	- CRM webhook endpoints verify HMAC signature per provider (`X-Hub-Signature-256` for Blackbaud-style, provider-specific headers for Tessitura/NeonOne/Salesforce)
22	- Staff override requires PIN check against `staff.pin` (column already exists, unused)
23	- Dashboard CORS lockdown: replace `allow_origins=["*"]` with institution-configured origin list
24	
25	**What to watch in the run:**
26	- **Contract path-invention:** will the negotiator correctly Glob `apps/dashboard/` and `supabase/functions/` to ground expected_files? rc3's phase-04 contract did this correctly; needs to hold on hybrid too.
27	- **Task granularity:** hybrid should produce smaller phases than greenfield (no scaffold needed). Expect 2-3 phases, not 5.
28	- **Bundling drift:** auth touches shared files (middleware.ts, every Edge Function, dashboard layout). This is the file-coupling pattern that caused phase-02 of rc3 to bundle. If rc4's atomic-commit discipline holds here, that's a real win.
29	- **Cost comparison:** should be dramatically cheaper than rc3's $87 — hybrid route skips scaffold + plan is smaller + no phase-01 boilerplate.
30	
31	**rc4 observability payoff:** the per-component `api_usage` events shipped in rc4 will let us see "which subagent cost the most" for the first time. Expect contract-negotiator + reviewer to dominate if they still pay the ~130K cache-read-per-call cost noted in rc3.
32	
33	**How to apply:** when the user signals "next dogfood" or "let's add auth", propose this as a clean hybrid-route test. Use the existing `cuseum-vam-full` repo at its current HEAD (`493b665` entropy commit). Write the auth spec fresh, don't reuse anything from the rc3 spec. Keep scope tight — auth + webhook signatures + PIN check only; resist the urge to also add rate limiting or observability in the same run.

---
name: Add auth via hybrid route on top of rc3 VAM ship — next dogfood
description: After rc4 observability ships, use dirigent's hybrid route on the existing cuseum-vam-full repo to layer auth onto the shipped code. Tests whether hybrid handles feature-addition on a dirigent-built codebase.
type: project
originSessionId: bb028fb8-27a3-4fc5-8c43-5208f3e988ba
---
**Queued for the dogfood after rc4.** User articulated 2026-04-19 during the post-rc3 debrief.

**The test:** the rc3 dogfood (greenfield route) shipped VAM without auth because the spec didn't name it. Rather than re-running greenfield with a new spec, re-enter the existing `cuseum-vam-full` repo with the **hybrid route** and a small auth-focused spec. Hybrid is the "feature work on an existing, actively maintained project" route — exactly this shape.

**Why this is a good test:**
- Hybrid route has never been dogfooded on a dirigent-built codebase. Usually the implicit assumption is hybrid runs on human-authored code. Running it on rc3's output is the more honest test — can dirigent *extend* its own prior work?
- The feature (auth) is additive, not refactor-heavy. Middleware + login page + JWT check in Edge Functions + webhook HMAC verification — all layerable on top of existing resolver and dashboard code.
- Confirms that `feedback_no_requirement_invention.md` is the right principle — the fix is to add the requirements and run again, not to teach dirigent to invent them.

**Spec to write (before running):**
Requirements should cover:
- Coordinator login via Supabase Auth (email + password) → dashboard session cookie
- Pro/Standard institution inferred from authenticated user → feeds existing tier-gating middleware (already shipped)
- Edge Functions require valid JWT (remove `--no-verify-jwt` from `supabase functions serve`, verify via `Deno.env.get('SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET')`)
- CRM webhook endpoints verify HMAC signature per provider (`X-Hub-Signature-256` for Blackbaud-style, provider-specific headers for Tessitura/NeonOne/Salesforce)
- Staff override requires PIN check against `staff.pin` (column already exists, unused)
- Dashboard CORS lockdown: replace `allow_origins=["*"]` with institution-configured origin list

**What to watch in the run:**
- **Contract path-invention:** will the negotiator correctly Glob `apps/dashboard/` and `supabase/functions/` to ground expected_files? rc3's phase-04 contract did this correctly; needs to hold on hybrid too.
- **Task granularity:** hybrid should produce smaller phases than greenfield (no scaffold needed). Expect 2-3 phases, not 5.
- **Bundling drift:** auth touches shared files (middleware.ts, every Edge Function, dashboard layout). This is the file-coupling pattern that caused phase-02 of rc3 to bundle. If rc4's atomic-commit discipline holds here, that's a real win.
- **Cost comparison:** should be dramatically cheaper than rc3's $87 — hybrid route skips scaffold + plan is smaller + no phase-01 boilerplate.

**rc4 observability payoff:** the per-component `api_usage` events shipped in rc4 will let us see "which subagent cost the most" for the first time. Expect contract-negotiator + reviewer to dominate if they still pay the ~130K cache-read-per-call cost noted in rc3.

**How to apply:** when the user signals "next dogfood" or "let's add auth", propose this as a clean hybrid-route test. Use the existing `cuseum-vam-full` repo at its current HEAD (`493b665` entropy commit). Write the auth spec fresh, don't reuse anything from the rc3 spec. Keep scope tight — auth + webhook signatures + PIN check only; resist the urge to also add rate limiting or observability in the same run.

Recalled. Summary of `project_hybrid_route_auth_followup.md`:

**The queued dogfood:** Re-enter the existing `cuseum-vam-full` repo (at HEAD `493b665`) via dirigent's **hybrid route** and layer auth on top. Tests whether hybrid handles feature-addition on a dirigent-built codebase — not just human-authored code.

**Why it's a good test:**
- Hybrid route never dogfooded on dirigent's own output before
- Auth is additive (middleware + login + JWT + HMAC + PIN + CORS), not refactor-heavy
- Confirms `feedback_no_requirement_invention` — fix is to add requirements and re-run, not teach dirigent to invent them

**Spec scope (tight — auth only):**
- Supabase Auth coordinator login → dashboard session cookie
- Pro/Standard tier inferred from authed user (feeds existing tier-gating)
- Edge Functions require JWT (drop `--no-verify-jwt`)
- CRM webhook HMAC verification per provider
- Staff PIN check against existing `staff.pin` column
- Dashboard CORS lockdown (replace `allow_origins=["*"]`)

**What to watch:**
- Contract path-invention (Glob grounding must hold on hybrid)
- Task granularity — expect 2-3 phases, not 5
- Bundling drift on shared files (middleware, every Edge Function, dashboard layout) — rc4 atomic-commit discipline's real test
- Cost vs rc3's $87 — should be dramatically cheaper
- rc4 `api_usage` per component will reveal which subagent dominates

**Resist:** adding rate limiting or observability in the same run.

---

Plan mode is active but there's no implementation to plan yet — just context recall. What would you like to do next? Kick off the dogfood, write the auth spec first, or something else?

1	# Lane 3 — The 6 Routes
2	
3	**Goal:** In one scannable table, show the user every execution route dirigent can auto-select, what triggers each, and a one-line example of when to use it. Then route them to the matching lane for hands-on.
4	
5	## Display this table
6	
7	| Route | Trigger condition | Phase sequence | Example use case | One-line CLI |
8	|---|---|---|---|---|
9	| **Quick** ⚡ | Small, self-contained change doable in 1-3 files | Plan → Execute → Ship | "Fix the typo in the welcome email subject line" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` (auto-routed) |
10	| **Greenfield** 🌱 | New or small repo, no existing tests, no complex conventions | Scaffold → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Scaffold a new FastAPI service with auth and a health endpoint" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` |
11	| **Legacy** 🏛️ | Established codebase with domain rules to preserve, migration-heavy work | Init → Extract Rules → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Migrate this Rails app's auth from Devise to a custom JWT middleware without losing business rules" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --use-proteus` |
12	| **Hybrid** 🧩 | Mid-sized repo where only part of the codebase is relevant to the change | Init → Quick Scan → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Add a new endpoint to an existing API without touching the frontend" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` (auto-routed) |
13	| **Testability** 🧪 | Repo has poor test coverage and you want to improve *before* adding features | Init → Testability Analysis → Plan → Execute → Ship | "Get this module to 80% coverage and remove the untested death zones" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --route testability` |
14	| **Tracking** 📊 | Feature is analytics/instrumentation, not behavior | Init → PostHog Setup → Plan → Execute → Ship | "Instrument the checkout flow with PostHog events for conversion tracking" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --route tracking` |
15	
16	## The selection logic
17	
18	Dirigent auto-detects the route from the repo state and the SPEC during the `Init` phase. You can override with `--route`, but the auto-detection is usually right:
19	
20	- **Tiny, self-contained change** → Quick
21	- **No tests + small codebase** → Greenfield
22	- **Domain rules + large/legacy codebase** → Legacy (with optional Proteus for deep extraction)
23	- **Well-bounded change in a mid-sized repo** → Hybrid
24	- **SPEC is mostly about "add coverage" or "make X testable"** → Testability
25	- **SPEC is mostly about events, analytics, or instrumentation** → Tracking
26	
27	## The prompt
28	
29	After showing the table, ask (via AskUserQuestion):
30	
31	> "Which one matches what you're trying to do today?"
32	
33	Options:
34	- **Quick — tiny self-contained change** → jump to Lane 2 (plan a real change) with the Quick hint
35	- **Greenfield — I'm building something new** → jump to Lane 2 (plan a real change) with the Greenfield hint
36	- **Legacy — I'm modifying an established codebase** → jump to Lane 2 with the Legacy hint (and surface `domain-context-beats-orchestration.md`)
37	- **Hybrid — I'm making a bounded change to an existing repo** → jump to Lane 2 with the Hybrid hint
38	- **Testability — I want to improve test coverage** → jump to Lane 2 (SPEC should be scoped to test coverage)
39	- **Tracking — I want to add analytics** → jump to Lane 2 with the Tracking hint
40	- **None of these / I'm not sure** → jump to Lane 4 (Playbook) with `when-not-to-use-dirigent.md` as the first read
41	
42	Whatever they pick, the answer is almost always "now let's write a SPEC in Lane 2." Lane 3 is primarily mental-model formation, not a terminal state.

file.content:
# Lane 3 — The 6 Routes

**Goal:** In one scannable table, show the user every execution route dirigent can auto-select, what triggers each, and a one-line example of when to use it. Then route them to the matching lane for hands-on.

## Display this table

| Route | Trigger condition | Phase sequence | Example use case | One-line CLI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quick** ⚡ | Small, self-contained change doable in 1-3 files | Plan → Execute → Ship | "Fix the typo in the welcome email subject line" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` (auto-routed) |
| **Greenfield** 🌱 | New or small repo, no existing tests, no complex conventions | Scaffold → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Scaffold a new FastAPI service with auth and a health endpoint" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` |
| **Legacy** 🏛️ | Established codebase with domain rules to preserve, migration-heavy work | Init → Extract Rules → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Migrate this Rails app's auth from Devise to a custom JWT middleware without losing business rules" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --use-proteus` |
| **Hybrid** 🧩 | Mid-sized repo where only part of the codebase is relevant to the change | Init → Quick Scan → Plan → Execute → Entropy Min → Ship | "Add a new endpoint to an existing API without touching the frontend" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo .` (auto-routed) |
| **Testability** 🧪 | Repo has poor test coverage and you want to improve *before* adding features | Init → Testability Analysis → Plan → Execute → Ship | "Get this module to 80% coverage and remove the untested death zones" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --route testability` |
| **Tracking** 📊 | Feature is analytics/instrumentation, not behavior | Init → PostHog Setup → Plan → Execute → Ship | "Instrument the checkout flow with PostHog events for conversion tracking" | `dirigent --spec SPEC.md --repo . --route tracking` |

## The selection logic

Dirigent auto-detects the route from the repo state and the SPEC during the `Init` phase. You can override with `--route`, but the auto-detection is usually right:

- **Tiny, self-contained change** → Quick
- **No tests + small codebase** → Greenfield
- **Domain rules + large/legacy codebase** → Legacy (with optional Proteus for deep extraction)
- **Well-bounded change in a mid-sized repo** → Hybrid
- **SPEC is mostly about "add coverage" or "make X testable"** → Testability
- **SPEC is mostly about events, analytics, or instrumentation** → Tracking

## The prompt

After showing the table, ask (via AskUserQuestion):

> "Which one matches what you're trying to do today?"

Options:
- **Quick — tiny self-contained change** → jump to Lane 2 (plan a real change) with the Quick hint
- **Greenfield — I'm building something new** → jump to Lane 2 (plan a real change) with the Greenfield hint
- **Legacy — I'm modifying an established codebase** → jump to Lane 2 with the Legacy hint (and surface `domain-context-beats-orchestration.md`)
- **Hybrid — I'm making a bounded change to an existing repo** → jump to Lane 2 with the Hybrid hint
- **Testability — I want to improve test coverage** → jump to Lane 2 (SPEC should be scoped to test coverage)
- **Tracking — I want to add analytics** → jump to Lane 2 with the Tracking hint
- **None of these / I'm not sure** → jump to Lane 4 (Playbook) with `when-not-to-use-dirigent.md` as the first read

Whatever they pick, the answer is almost always "now let's write a SPEC in Lane 2." Lane 3 is primarily mental-model formation, not a terminal state.

lastPrompt: what steps does hybrid route have

content.text:
Hybrid route steps (from `src/outbid_dirigent/router.py:179-218`):

| # | Step | Required | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Init** | optional | Bootstrap dev env, seed data, e2e creds |
| 2 | **Quick Scan** | required | Claude scans only files relevant to the feature |
| 3 | **Planning** | required | Build plan with repo context |
| 4 | **Execution** | required | Run tasks with repo context |
| 5 | **Entropy Minimization** | optional | Align docs, remove dead code, resolve contradictions |
| 6 | **Test Suite** | optional | Run full test suite from test manifest |
| 7 | **Ship** | required | Branch, push, PR |

Difference vs greenfield: no **Scaffold** (you're working on existing code) and adds **Quick Scan** (narrow the blast radius to feature-relevant files only).

Difference vs legacy: no **Extract Rules** / Proteus step — hybrid assumes the relevant slice of code is small enough that focused context beats full domain extraction.

1	---
2	name: quick-scan
3	description: Quick scan of relevant files for a feature (Hybrid route)
4	context: fork
5	agent: infra-architect
6	---
7	
8	# Quick Scan
9	
10	You identify the **minimal set of files** relevant to the feature described in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` and write `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` for the planner to consume.
11	
12	This is the Hybrid route's distinguishing step — the sweet-spot between Greenfield (no prior code) and Legacy (extract every rule). The repo is mid-size and only a slice is relevant. Read that slice **smart**, not all.
13	
14	Your output is the planner's fuel: if you miss the entry point, the plan will route tasks to the wrong files. If you dump the whole repo, the planner drowns in tokens and writes bad tasks.
15	
16	## When This Runs
17	
18	Hybrid route, after `run-init`. You have:
19	- `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md` — what's being built
20	- `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` — build/test/run commands (from init)
21	- The live repo — read it selectively
22	
23	You are consumed by `create-plan`, which reads `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` at Step 1 and uses it to skip generic repo analysis.
24	
25	## Steps At A Glance
26	
27	| # | Step | Output |
28	|---|---|---|
29	| 1 | Read SPEC and pull feature keywords | Keyword list in memory |
30	| 2 | Grep by feature name + related terms | Candidate file list |
31	| 3 | Identify entry points and follow imports (depth ≤ 2) | Entry points + direct deps |
32	| 4 | Find nearest sibling feature (pattern reference) | 1-2 files to mirror |
33	| 5 | Classify: modify vs. read-only vs. ignore | Triaged file list |
34	| 6 | Extract 2-4 pattern snippets | Snippets in memory |
35	| 7 | Write CONTEXT.md | `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` |
36	| 8 | Validate size budget | Under token ceiling |
37	
38	No commit. CONTEXT.md is transient planner fuel in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}`, not repo content.
39	
40	## Step 1: Extract Feature Keywords from SPEC
41	
42	Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md`. Pull two kinds of terms:
43	
44	- **Feature name tokens** — nouns and verbs from the feature title (e.g. "password reset" → `password`, `reset`, `forgot`)
45	- **Domain/adjacent terms** — entities and actions mentioned in the SPEC body (e.g. `user`, `email`, `token`, `expiry`)
46	
47	Write these as a mental list. You will use them in Step 2.
48	
49	## Step 2: Grep by Feature Name and Related Terms
50	
51	Search in this order — stop as soon as you have a clear entry point:
52	
53	1. **Exact feature name.** `rg -l "password_reset|PasswordReset|forgotPassword"` — case variants covering snake, Pascal, and camel.
54	2. **Domain terms in combination.** `rg -l "reset.*token|token.*reset"` — narrows to the specific intersection.
55	3. **Related HTTP surface.** `rg -l "POST.*reset|/auth/reset|route.*reset"` if the feature touches the API layer.
56	4. **Adjacent feature by analogy.** If the feature is "password reset" and the repo has "email verification", the verification files are your pattern reference. `rg -l "email.*verif"`.
57	
58	**Polyglot note.** Constrain by language of the affected layer: `--type py`, `--type ts`, `--type rb`. The infra-architect agent already knows the repo's languages from `ANALYSIS.json` / `test-harness.json`. Do not grep binary files, lockfiles, or `dist/` output.
59	
60	## Step 3: Identify Entry Points, Follow Imports Shallowly
61	
62	From Step 2's candidates, pick **entry points** — the files a request/call/event first hits. Typical entry points:
63	
64	- HTTP routes (`routes.py`, `app/controllers/`, `pages/api/`, `src/routes/`)
65	- CLI handlers (`cli.py`, `bin/`, `commands/`)
66	- Event handlers (`handlers/`, `consumers/`, `workers/`)
67	- Framework-specific (`page.tsx`, `+page.svelte`, Rails actions)
68	
69	From each entry point, follow imports **one or two levels deep**. Stop there. Do not transitively chase every helper — the planner does not need the full graph.
70	
71	**Rule of thumb:** if a file is 3 hops from the entry point, it is almost certainly not relevant to this feature.
72	
73	## Step 4: Find a Pattern Reference (Sibling Feature)
74	
75	Find one feature already in the repo that solves an analogous problem. This is the **pattern reference** — the planner will tell the coder to mirror it.
76	
77	Examples:
78	- New CRUD endpoint → find an existing CRUD endpoint in the same framework
79	- New background job → find an existing job in the same queue system
80	- New React form → find an existing form component with validation
81	
82	Usually 1-2 files. If nothing analogous exists, note that in CONTEXT.md under "Pattern Reference" as "none — new pattern for this repo."
83	
84	## Step 5: Classify Files — Modify vs. Read-Only vs. Ignore
85	
86	Bucket every candidate into one of three:
87	
88	| Bucket | Meaning | Example |
89	|---|---|---|
90	| **Modify** | Feature cannot ship without changing this file | `routes.py` where the new endpoint lives |
91	| **Read-only** | Must be understood but not changed | `models/User.py` if you import it unchanged |
92	| **Ignore** | Matched your grep but unrelated | Old migration mentioning "reset" in a comment |
93	
94	Explicitly drop the ignore bucket. Do **not** include files you searched and rejected — that wastes planner tokens.
95	
96	## Step 6: Extract 2-4 Pattern Snippets
97	
98	From the pattern-reference files, copy **short** snippets (5-15 lines each) that demonstrate the conventions the new feature must follow. Good snippets show:
99	
100	- How errors are raised and caught
101	- How config/env is accessed
102	- How the return shape / response envelope looks
103	- How tests are structured (one test function as a template)
104	
105	Do **not** copy full files. Do **not** copy more than ~60 lines total across all snippets.
106	
107	## Step 7: Write CONTEXT.md
108	
109	Create `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` with exactly these sections:
110	
111	```markdown
112	# Context for Feature: {feature name from SPEC}
113	
114	## Entry Points
115	{The file(s) where a request/call/event for this feature first lands.
116	 One line per entry point with path and 1-sentence role.}
117	
118	- `src/routes/auth.py:L42` — POST handler for `/auth/reset` (to be added here)
119	
120	## Files to Modify
121	{Files that MUST change to ship the feature. Path + one-liner per file.
122	 If the file does not yet exist, mark as NEW.}
123	
124	- `src/routes/auth.py` — add `POST /auth/reset` endpoint
125	- `src/services/password.py` (NEW) — token generation + hashing logic
126	- `tests/test_auth.py` — add reset flow tests
127	
128	## Files to Read (Dependencies, do NOT modify)
129	{Files the coder must understand to modify the above, but will not change.
130	 Path + why it matters.}
131	
132	- `src/models/user.py` — User model with `email` and `password_hash` columns
133	- `src/services/email.py` — `send_email(to, template, ctx)` helper used for delivery
134	- `src/config.py` — `TOKEN_TTL_MINUTES` setting
135	
136	## Pattern Reference
137	{One analogous feature already in this repo. Planner tells coder to mirror it.
138	 Empty if none — say so explicitly.}
139	
140	Mirror: `src/routes/auth.py` `POST /auth/verify-email` (lines 78-120) — same
141	token-issue → email-send → confirm-via-link shape.
142	
143	## Pattern Snippets
144	{2-4 short snippets (5-15 lines each) showing conventions to follow.
145	 Error handling, config access, response shape, test shape.}
146	
147	### Error raising (from src/routes/auth.py)
148	```python
149	if not user:
150	    raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="user_not_found")
151	```
152	
153	### Response envelope (from src/routes/auth.py)
154	```python
155	return {"ok": True, "data": {...}}
156	```
157	
158	### Test shape (from tests/test_auth.py)
159	```python
160	def test_verify_email_happy_path(client, user_factory):
161	    user = user_factory()
162	    resp = client.post("/auth/verify-email", json={"token": "..."})
163	    assert resp.status_code == 200
164	```
165	
166	## Integration Points
167	{Where the new feature wires into existing code. Specific call sites,
168	 not generic statements.}
169	
170	- `src/routes/auth.py` — register new route in the existing `router = APIRouter()` block at L14
171	- `src/services/email.py` — call `send_email(user.email, "password_reset", {token})`
172	- `src/models/user.py` — no schema change; reuse `password_hash` column
173	
174	## Out of Scope for This Scan
175	{Things you looked at and deliberately did not include, with reason.
176	 Keeps the planner from re-discovering rejected paths.}
177	
178	- Admin password-force-reset (separate feature, different route)
179	- OAuth flows in `src/routes/oauth.py` (not password-related)
180	```
181	
182	### What does NOT go in CONTEXT.md
183	
184	- `README.md`, top-level docs, ADRs — unless a doc contains a pattern rule the coder must follow
185	- `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `.venv/`, `vendor/`, `.git/`, `__pycache__/`
186	- Lockfiles (`package-lock.json`, `uv.lock`, `Gemfile.lock`)
187	- Generated code (`*.pb.go`, `schema.graphql` generated clients, OpenAPI generated types)
188	- Migrations older than the current feature's history, unless the feature changes schema
189	- Test fixtures unless they are the pattern reference
190	- Full file bodies — only short snippets
191	
192	## Step 8: Validate Size Budget
193	
194	CONTEXT.md must be **lean**. Hard ceilings:
195	
196	| Metric | Budget | Hard cap |
197	|---|---|---|
198	| Total lines | ≤ 200 | 300 |
199	| Total tokens | ≤ 3k | 5k |
200	| Files listed (Modify + Read) | ≤ 15 | 25 |
201	| Pattern snippets | 2-4 | 6 |
202	
203	If you exceed the hard cap, the scan is not "quick" anymore and the planner will drown. Trim the Read-only bucket first, then drop borderline snippets.
204	
205	Check with:
206	```bash
207	wc -l "${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md"
208	```
209	
210	**Stopping rule.** Do not spend more than 10 minutes scanning. If after 10 minutes you still cannot identify an entry point or pattern reference, write what you have and note the uncertainty in "Out of Scope for This Scan". The planner will handle the gap.
211	
212	## Anti-Patterns
213	
214	<anti-patterns>
215	| Anti-pattern | Why it fails | Do instead |
216	|---|---|---|
217	| `rg "."` and list everything that matched | Planner drowns; no signal | Grep for feature-specific terms from Step 1 |
218	| Paste full file bodies | Blows the token budget, defeats the "quick" in quick-scan | 5-15 line snippets from the pattern reference only |
219	| Include `README.md` as a dependency | README is prose, not pattern; coder won't learn from it | Extract the concrete convention into a snippet |
220	| Follow imports to depth 5+ | Transitive chase pulls in the whole graph | Stop at depth 2 from entry point |
221	| List tests in "Files to Read" | Tests aren't dependencies; they're pattern templates | Put one test function as a snippet under "Pattern Snippets" |
222	| Include vendored / generated / lockfile paths | Coder never edits them; wastes planner attention | Exclusion list in Step 7 |
223	| Skip the sibling-feature pattern reference | Coder reinvents conventions; entropy goes up | Always find one, or explicitly say "none" |
224	| Write CONTEXT.md without classifying (mixed bucket) | Planner can't distinguish modify from read | Step 5 three-bucket classification, drop Ignore |
225	| Scan every language in a polyglot repo | 80% of the hits are irrelevant | Constrain `--type` to the feature's layer |
226	| Commit CONTEXT.md to the repo | It's transient planner fuel, not docs | Lives in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}`, no commit step |
227	</anti-patterns>
228	
229	## Rules
230	
231	<rules>
232	<rule>Scan smart, not exhaustive — if you're reading more than 15 files, you've left Hybrid territory and should have taken the Legacy route</rule>
233	<rule>Every entry in "Files to Modify" must have a one-sentence reason tied to the SPEC — no file listed without justification</rule>
234	<rule>Always name one Pattern Reference (analogous existing feature) or explicitly write "none — new pattern for this repo"</rule>
235	<rule>Pattern snippets are 5-15 lines each, total ≤ 60 lines across all snippets — never paste full files</rule>
236	<rule>Constrain grep by language (--type) based on the feature's layer — do not scan the whole polyglot tree</rule>
237	<rule>Follow imports at most 2 levels deep from entry points — transitive chase is out of scope</rule>
238	<rule>Do NOT include tests in "Files to Read" — tests belong as pattern snippets, not dependencies</rule>
239	<rule>Exclude node_modules, dist, build, vendor, .venv, .git, __pycache__, lockfiles, and generated code — never list them</rule>
240	<rule>README and top-level docs do NOT go in CONTEXT.md unless they encode a hard convention the coder must follow — extract the convention as a snippet instead</rule>
241	<rule>CONTEXT.md is transient — lives in ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}, never committed, no start.sh, no test-harness.json produced here</rule>
242	<rule>If you cannot find an entry point within 10 minutes, write what you have and note the gap in "Out of Scope for This Scan" — do not stall</rule>
243	<rule>Match CONTEXT.md's sections to what create-plan reads (Entry Points, Files to Modify, Files to Read, Pattern Reference, Pattern Snippets, Integration Points, Out of Scope) — the planner relies on these names</rule>
244	</rules>
245	
246	<constraints>
247	<constraint>Output: `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` only — no repo files, no commit</constraint>
248	<constraint>Maximum 10 minutes of scanning</constraint>
249	<constraint>CONTEXT.md ≤ 200 lines / ≤ 3k tokens (hard cap 300 lines / 5k tokens)</constraint>
250	<constraint>Files listed ≤ 15 (hard cap 25) — if you need more, you're in Legacy territory, not Hybrid</constraint>
251	</constraints>
252

### What does NOT go in CONTEXT.md

- `README.md`, top-level docs, ADRs — unless a doc contains a pattern rule the coder must follow
- `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `.venv/`, `vendor/`, `.git/`, `__pycache__/`

- Test fixtures unless they are the pattern reference
- Full file bodies — only short snippets

## Step 8: Validate Size Budget

CONTEXT.md must be **lean**. Hard ceilings:

| Metric | Budget | Hard cap |
|---|---|---|
| Total lines | ≤ 200 | 300 |
| Total tokens | ≤ 3k | 5k |
| Files listed (Modify + Read) | ≤ 15 | 25 |
| Pattern snippets | 2-4 | 6 |

If you exceed the hard cap, the scan is not "quick" anymore and the planner will drown. Trim the Read-only bucket first, then drop borderline snippets.

Check with:
```bash

```

**Stopping rule.** Do not spend more than 10 minutes scanning. If after 10 minutes you still cannot identify an entry point or pattern reference, write what you have and note the uncertainty in "Out of Scope for This Scan". The planner will handle the gap.

## Anti-Patterns

<anti-patterns>
| Anti-pattern | Why it fails | Do instead |
|---|---|---|
| `rg "."` and list everything that matched | Planner drowns; no signal | Grep for feature-specific terms from Step 1 |
| Paste full file bodies | Blows the token budget, defeats the "quick" in quick-scan | 5-15 line snippets from the pattern reference only |
| Include `README.md` as a dependency | README is prose, not pattern; coder won't learn from it | Extract the concrete convention into a snippet |
| Follow imports to depth 5+ | Transitive chase pulls in the whole graph | Stop at depth 2 from entry point |
| List tests in "Files to Read" | Tests aren't dependencies; they're pattern templates | Put one test function as a snippet under "Pattern Snippets" |
| Include vendored / generated / lockfile paths | Coder never edits them; wastes planner attention | Exclusion list in Step 7 |
| Skip the sibling-feature pattern reference | Coder reinvents conventions; entropy goes up | Always find one, or explicitly say "none" |
| Write CONTEXT.md without classifying (mixed bucket) | Planner can't distinguish modify from read | Step 5 three-bucket classification, drop Ignore |
| Scan every language in a polyglot repo | 80% of the hits are irrelevant | Constrain `--type` to the feature's layer |
| Commit CONTEXT.md to the repo | It's transient planner fuel, not docs | Lives in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}`, no commit step |
</anti-patterns>

## Rules

<rules>
<rule>Scan smart, not exhaustive — if you're reading more than 15 files, you've left Hybrid territory and should have taken the Legacy route</rule>
<rule>Every entry in "Files to Modify" must have a one-sentence reason tied to the SPEC — no file listed without justification</rule>
<rule>Always name one Pattern Reference (analogous existing feature) or explicitly write "none — new pattern for this repo"</rule>
<rule>Pattern snippets are 5-15 lines each, total ≤ 60 lines across all snippets — never paste full files</rule>
<rule>Constrain grep by language (--type) based on the feature's layer — do not scan the whole polyglot tree</rule>
<rule>Follow imports at most 2 levels deep from entry points — transitive chase is out of scope</rule>
<rule>Do NOT include tests in "Files to Read" — tests belong as pattern snippets, not dependencies</rule>
<rule>Exclude node_modules, dist, build, vendor, .venv, .git, __pycache__, lockfiles, and generated code — never list them</rule>
<rule>README and top-level docs do NOT go in CONTEXT.md unless they encode a hard convention the coder must follow — extract the convention as a snippet instead</rule>
<rule>CONTEXT.md is transient — lives in ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}, never committed, no start.sh, no test-harness.json produced here</rule>
<rule>If you cannot find an entry point within 10 minutes, write what you have and note the gap in "Out of Scope for This Scan" — do not stall</rule>
<rule>Match CONTEXT.md's sections to what create-plan reads (Entry Points, Files to Modify, Files to Read, Pattern Reference, Pattern Snippets, Integration Points, Out of Scope) — the planner relies on these names</rule>
</rules>

<constraints>
<constraint>Output: `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` only — no repo files, no commit</constraint>
<constraint>Maximum 10 minutes of scanning</constraint>
<constraint>CONTEXT.md ≤ 200 lines / ≤ 3k tokens (hard cap 300 lines / 5k tokens)</constraint>
<constraint>Files listed ≤ 15 (hard cap 25) — if you need more, you're in Legacy territory, not Hybrid</constraint>
</constraints>

# Engineering Standards

You are working in a repository governed by portfolio engineering standards.
The full standards document is at `harness-docs/engineering-standards.md` — read it
when a skill or agent needs standards context.

## How to read this document

- **[ENFORCED]** — Non-negotiable. All code, PRs, and architectural decisions
  MUST comply. If you cannot comply, stop and explain the conflict to the developer.
- **[RECOMMENDED]** — Follow when practical. Note when you choose not to follow
  a recommendation and briefly explain why.

## Deviation protocol

If a developer explicitly instructs you to deviate from an [ENFORCED] standard,
comply with their instruction but add a comment or note in the PR description
flagging the deviation and the reason given.

## Templates

Document templates are available in `harness-docs/templates/`. When creating ADRs,
PIRs, runbooks, or working agreements, always use the corresponding template.

## Universal Rules

These rules apply to every interaction. No exceptions unless the developer
explicitly authorises a deviation.

### [ENFORCED]

1. **No secrets in code.** Never commit API keys, tokens, passwords, or credentials.
   Use environment variables or a secrets manager. If a secret is needed, direct the
   user to inject it — do not generate it inline.
2. **Conventional Commits.** All commit messages use `<type>(<scope>): <description>`
   where type is one of: feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, ci, perf, or
   BREAKING CHANGE.
3. **Tests with new code.** New functionality gets corresponding tests in the same
   changeset. Do not skip tests and do not write tests solely to satisfy a coverage
   gate for code you also generated.
4. **Small, focused PRs.** One logical change per PR. Every PR description includes:
   what changed, why, how to test it, and a link to the related ticket.
5. **Peer review required.** At least one peer review before merge to main. No
   self-merging of feature work.
6. **Feature toggles for new capabilities.** User-facing features ship behind a
   toggle by default.
7. **Structured logging.** Emit logs in JSON format with consistent fields:
   timestamp, service, level, correlation/trace ID, and message.
8. **Flag AI-generated code.** Note in the PR description when AI assistance
   constitutes a significant portion of the change.
9. **Confirm before irreversible actions.** Force-push, file deletion, table drops,
   production API calls, and deployment require explicit user authorisation in this
   session.
10. **Use templates for documents.** ADRs, PIRs, runbooks, and working agreements
    must use the corresponding template from `harness-docs/templates/`.
11. **Standards review before committing.** Before any `git commit`, invoke
    the `standards-reviewer` agent on the staged diff. Do not commit if any
    [MUST] requirement fails. If there are no applicable standards for the
    changes, proceed.

## When to Suggest Skills

When you notice one of these situations during regular work, mention the relevant
skill **once**. Do not repeat if the user declines or ignores the suggestion.

| Situation | Suggest |
|---|---|
| No tests in repo or near changed code | `/test-bootstrap` |
| No CI pipeline detected | `/add-ci` |
| No README or README is sparse | `/document-codebase` |
| Architectural decision being made, no ADR exists | `/adr` |
| No ARCHITECTURE.md exists or an agent needs a codebase overview | `/generate-architecture` |
| Need a project-specific Claude Code plugin / .claude config | `/create-plugin` |
| User wants a repo health check or compliance audit | `/assess` |
| Ad-hoc boolean flags controlling feature access | `/feature-toggle-audit` |
| No structured logging in service code | `/add-structured-logging` |
| No dependency/CVE scanning in CI | `/add-security-scan` |
| Security concern in code under review | `/security-audit` |
| Code smells or outdated dependencies noticed | `/tech-debt` |
| Complex file needs safe step-by-step refactoring | `/refactor-plan` |
| Onboarding someone to the codebase | `/codebase-tour` |
| Developer wants to run the repo locally / no local-dev-setup.md exists | `/setup-dev` |
| Incident occurred, need a post-incident review | `/pir` |
| No release automation or version bumping | `/add-release` |
| Need analytics event tracking schema | `/tracking-plan` |
| No feature toggle system present | `/add-feature-toggle` |
| Need to set up working agreement or agentic standards | `/setup-agentic-standards` |
| About to commit code with changes | `standards-reviewer` agent |

### Proactive behaviour

- If you write new code and notice no tests exist nearby, mention `/test-bootstrap`
  once — do not repeat.
- When a session ends with new code but no tests added, suggest `/test-bootstrap`
  rather than just noting the gap.
- Use `/assess` as the entry point when a developer asks "where should we start?"

## Stack: TypeScript

### [ENFORCED] Code quality

- Use `prettier` for formatting and `eslint` for linting.
  Configuration MUST be committed to the repository.
- Enable `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json`. Do not use `any` unless there
  is a documented reason. Prefer `unknown` for truly unknown types.
- Use ESM imports (`import`/`export`) not CommonJS (`require`/`module.exports`)
  unless the project has a documented reason for CommonJS.
- Prefer `const` over `let`. Never use `var`.

### [ENFORCED] Testing

- Use `vitest` (preferred) or `jest` as the test framework.
- Co-locate test files with source files (`*.test.ts`, `*.spec.ts`)
  or in a parallel `__tests__/` directory — be consistent within the project.
- Use `@testing-library` for component testing in React/Vue projects.
- Mock external dependencies at the boundary (API clients, not internal modules).

### [ENFORCED] Dependency management

- Use a lockfile (`package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, or `pnpm-lock.yaml`).
  Never edit lock files directly.
- Prefer `devDependencies` for build/test tools. Keep `dependencies` minimal.
- Pin exact versions for critical dependencies.

### [ENFORCED] Release tooling

- Use `semantic-release` for automated version bumping and changelog generation.
  Run `/add-release` to set up the workflow and config.
- Install release plugins as `devDependencies`:
  `@semantic-release/changelog`, `@semantic-release/git`, `@semantic-release/github`.
- Never manually edit `CHANGELOG.md` or bump the version in `package.json` —
  these are managed by the release pipeline.

### [ENFORCED] Logging

- Use a structured logging library (e.g. `pino`, `winston` with JSON transport).
- Never use `console.log` for production logging. `console.log` is acceptable
  only in development/debugging contexts.
- Include correlation IDs in all log entries for request tracing.

### [ENFORCED] Code intelligence setup

The `typescript-lsp` plugin is pre-enabled for this project. Install the required binary:

```sh
npm install -g typescript typescript-language-server
```

Then run `/reload-plugins` in Claude Code. Verify with `/plugin` → Installed tab.

### [RECOMMENDED] Maturity milestones

- Use `typescript-eslint` with strict type-checked rules.
- Enforce import sorting with `eslint-plugin-import` or `prettier-plugin-organize-imports`.
- Use `knip` or `ts-prune` to detect unused exports and dead code.

---

## Working with these standards

- The full engineering standards are in `harness-docs/engineering-standards.md`. Read it
  when you need detailed requirements for a specific area.
- When creating documents (ADRs, PIRs, runbooks, working agreements), check
  `harness-docs/templates/` for the corresponding template first.
- When uncertain whether a standard applies to the current context, ask the
  developer rather than guessing.
- These standards are a living document. If you identify a gap or conflict,
  flag it to the developer for discussion.

<!-- END:bid-harness -->

# Engineering Standards

**Portfolio Company Engineering Playbook** *Version 1.0 · April 2026*

This standard bootstraps Claude Code into any git repository as enforceable engineering guidelines for both developers and AI agents.

---

## Purpose

A common foundation of engineering excellence across all portfolio companies — enabling speed, quality, and scalability.

## Scope

Applies to all engineering teams. Standards are marked **[MUST]** (baseline required) or **[RECOMMENDED]** (maturity milestones).

---

## 01 · Culture & Working Agreements

### Working Agreements

**[MUST]**

- Maintain a written Working Agreement covering: meeting cadences, communication norms, Definition of Done, on-call expectations, and decision-making process.
- Apply a Definition of Done (DoD) to every story before marking it complete. DoD must include: code reviewed, tests passing, feature toggled if needed, monitoring in place.
- Run retrospectives at least bi-weekly. Track action items with an owner and due date.

**[RECOMMENDED]**

- Apply a Definition of Done (DoD) to every story before marking it complete. DoD must include: code reviewed, tests passing, feature toggled if needed, monitoring in place.
- Run retrospectives at least bi-weekly. Track action items with an owner and due date.

**[RECOMMENDED]**

lastPrompt: what is quick scan doing/looking at? specifically, can we tell it to look at files that our coding standards harness enforces (not part of this codebase but see the cuseum-vam-full repo, i let it run…

content.text:
**What quick-scan does** (from `src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/quick-scan/SKILL.md`):

1. Reads `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/SPEC.md`, pulls feature keywords
2. Greps the repo for those keywords (constrained by language, excludes `node_modules`, `dist`, lockfiles, generated code)
3. Follows imports ≤2 hops from entry points
4. Finds one sibling feature as a "pattern reference"
5. Extracts 2-4 short snippets (5-15 lines each)
6. Writes `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/CONTEXT.md` with fixed sections: Entry Points / Files to Modify / Files to Read / Pattern Reference / Pattern Snippets / Integration Points / Out of Scope
7. Hard caps: ≤200 lines, ≤3k tokens, ≤15 files

**On your standards harness.** I see cuseum-vam-full has:
- `harness-docs/engineering-standards.md` + `remediation-plan.md` + `standards-status.md`
- `.claude/plugins/bid-harness/` injecting the standards block into `CLAUDE.md` between `<!-- BEGIN:bid-harness -->` / `<!-- END:bid-harness -->`
- `standards-reviewer` and `compliance-checker` agents
- A rule: "invoke `standards-reviewer` on staged diff before any `git commit`"

**Can quick-scan pick those up today?** Not as a first-class input. The SKILL.md explicitly says "README, top-level docs, ADRs — do NOT include unless they encode a hard convention the coder must follow," and there's no section in CONTEXT.md's schema for "enforced conventions." There's no `--conventions-docs` flag.

**Three ways to get the harness into dirigent's brain anyway:**

1. **Cite the standards in the SPEC.** The auth SPEC's constraints section says *"MUST comply with `harness-docs/engineering-standards.md`; commits MUST pass `standards-reviewer`"*. Planner reads SPEC directly → constraints flow to every task. Cleanest, no code change.

2. **Rely on CLAUDE.md inheritance.** The bid-harness block is already in `CLAUDE.md`. All Claude Code subagents (including quick-scan's `infra-architect`) inherit it — so the standards *are* in scope, they just aren't written into CONTEXT.md explicitly. Risk: subagents may drop CLAUDE.md context when compacting.

3. **Extend quick-scan with an "Enforced Conventions" section.** Glob `harness-docs/*.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `.cursor/rules/*`, `standards/*.md`; cite paths + 3-5 line rule excerpts. Real skill edit.

**Watch out for:** dirigent's own `reviewer` agent vs the harness's `standards-reviewer`. Two reviewers may fight over the commit gate — worth deciding in the SPEC which authority wins, or have the harness's reviewer run as a dirigent verification command.

My recommendation for the auth dogfood: **option 1** first (SPEC-side). If that passes through planner→executor intact, you don't need the skill edit. If it gets dropped, that's the signal to do option 3.

content.content:
1	# Visitor Access Management (VAM) — Prototype Spec
2	
3	*Ein Web-Prototyp, der den Kernmoment beweist: Credential scannen → Kontext sehen → Entscheidung treffen → Event loggen. Alles andere ist simuliert, weggelassen oder zu Text-Input vereinfacht.*
4	
5	**Status:** Draft · **Scope:** 3–4 Wochen · **Demo-User:** 1 Institution
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1 — Prototype-Philosophie
10	
11	Ziel ist **nicht** ein Feature-vollständiges Produkt. Ziel ist der Beweis, dass der Datenfluss vom physischen Scan bis ins Dashboard funktioniert und der Membership-Koordinator zum ersten Mal sieht, was bisher unsichtbar war.
12	
13	> **Leitsatz:** Tabellen und Kerninteraktion nah an Production. Hardware → Text-Input. Integrationen → CSV-Upload. Mobile → Web. Multi-User → Demo-User. Multi-Institution → eine Institution.
14	
15	| Production-Realität | Prototype-Ersatz | |
16	|---|---|---|
17	| Kamera-Scanner (iOS/Android) | Text-Input: Token manuell eingeben oder aus Liste wählen | Simuliert |
18	| NFC-Tap / Wallet-Scan | Gleicher Text-Input | Simuliert |
19	| CRM-Sync (Blackbaud, Tessitura …) | CSV-Upload für Member-Import; kein Live-Sync | Simuliert |
20	| Multi-Station, Multi-Staff | Ein Demo-User, eine Station-ID hardcoded | Weggelassen |
21	| Post-Visit SMS (2–6h Delay) | Button „SMS senden" loggt Intent, zeigt Preview | Simuliert |
22	| Network Reporting (NARM/ASTC) | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
23	| Pricing / Feature Flags | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
24	| Offline-Mode | Nicht enthalten | Weggelassen |
25	| Datenmodell (Guests, Visits, Members) | **Production-nah** — echte Tabellen, echte Relationen | Production |
26	| Credential Resolution Engine | **Production-nah** — Token → Typ → Entitlement → Outcome | Production |
27	| Entry Dashboard | **Production-nah** — Live-Daten aus Visit-Tabelle | Production |
28	
29	---
30	
31	## 2 — Datenmodell (Production-Fundament)
32	
33	Diese Tabellen sind das Fundament. Sie werden im Prototyp exakt so gebaut, wie sie später in Production stehen — Felder, Typen, Relationen. Was hier steht, wird nicht weggeworfen.
34	
35	### members
36	
37	Import via CSV. Repräsentiert den CRM-Stand der Institution.
38	
39	```
40	members
41	├── id                    uuid        PK
42	├── institution_id        uuid        FK → institutions (hardcoded im Prototyp)
43	├── external_crm_id       string      ID aus Blackbaud/Tessitura/etc.
44	├── name                  string
45	├── email                 string
46	├── phone                 string      nullable
47	├── membership_tier       enum        basic | family | patron | vip
48	├── membership_status     enum        active | expired | lapsed
49	├── membership_expires_at date
50	├── guest_pass_total      int         pro Membership-Periode
51	├── guest_pass_used       int
52	├── reciprocal_networks   string[]    ["NARM", "ASTC"]
53	├── dm_token              string      unique — Barcode auf Digital Membership
54	└── imported_at           timestamp
55	```
56	
57	### guests
58	
59	Netto-neue Entität. Existiert heute nirgendwo in Cuseum.
60	
61	```
62	guests
63	├── id                    uuid        PK
64	├── guest_token           string      unique — Barcode auf Guest Pass
65	├── referrer_member_id    uuid        FK → members
66	├── phone                 string      captured bei Wallet-Add
67	├── wallet_added_at       timestamp
68	├── institution_id        uuid
69	├── status                enum        active | consumed | expired
70	└── created_at            timestamp
71	```
72	
73	### visits
74	
75	Kern-Audit-Trail. Jeder Scan-Event wird hier geschrieben.
76	
77	```
78	visits
79	├── id                    uuid        PK
80	├── token                 string      gescannter Barcode
81	├── credential_type       enum        dm_pass | guest_pass | day_ticket
82	├── member_id             uuid        nullable — FK → members
83	├── guest_id              uuid        nullable — FK → guests
84	├── outcome               enum        admit | deny | override
85	├── override_reason       string      nullable — Pflicht bei override
86	├── fraud_flags           string[]    ["duplicate_scan", "expired_override"]
87	├── station_id            string      hardcoded im Prototyp
88	├── staff_id              string      hardcoded im Prototyp
89	├── institution_id        uuid
90	├── scanned_at            timestamp
91	└── sms_triggered_at      timestamp   nullable
92	```
93	
94	### fraud_rules (Konfigurationstabelle)
95	
96	```
97	fraud_rules
98	├── id                    uuid
99	├── rule_type             enum        duplicate_scan | expired_override | guest_reuse | token_mismatch
100	├── window_minutes        int         z.B. 30 min für Duplicate Detection
101	├── severity              enum        info | warning | critical
102	└── active                boolean
103	```
104	
105	---
106	
107	## 3 — Die Kerninteraktion: Scan → Resolve → Decide → Log
108	
109	Das ist der eine Moment, der den Wert beweist. Alles andere im Prototyp existiert, um diesen Flow zu ermöglichen oder sein Ergebnis sichtbar zu machen.
110	
111	**01 — Token eingeben.** Der User tippt einen Barcode-String in ein Textfeld (simuliert den Kamera-Scan). Alternative: Dropdown mit vorhandenen Tokens aus der Demo-Datenbank.
112	
113	**02 — Credential Resolution.** Das System identifiziert den Token-Typ (DM-Pass, Guest Pass, Day Ticket), schlägt die zugehörige Member- oder Guest-Entität nach und prüft Entitlement: Mitgliedschaft aktiv? Guest Pass noch nicht consumed? Duplicate Scan innerhalb des Zeitfensters?
114	
115	**03 — Outcome-Anzeige mit Kontext.** Drei Zustände — grün / gelb / rot — jeweils mit spezifischem Kontext und empfohlener Aktion. Kein nacktes „Pass/Fail" mehr.
116	
117	**04 — Admit / Override / Deny.** Ein Klick schreibt den Visit-Record. Bei Override: Pflicht-Dropdown für Reason. Bei Guest Pass: Status wechselt zu „consumed".
118	
119	**05 — Fraud Detection.** Parallel zur Resolution prüft die Engine: Wurde dieser Token in den letzten X Minuten bereits gescannt? Wird ein expired Pass mit Override durchgewunken? Wird ein Guest Pass ein zweites Mal verwendet? Flags werden in `visits.fraud_flags` geschrieben.
120	
121	**06 — Dashboard-Update.** Der neue Visit-Record erscheint sofort im Entry Dashboard — Live-Counter, Credential-Breakdown, Flags-Queue.
122	
123	### Outcome-Zustände
124	
125	**✓ ADMIT (grün)** — Membership aktiv, Guest Pass gültig, oder Day Ticket valid. Kontext: Name, Tier, verbleibende Guest Passes.
126	
127	**⚠ REVIEW (gelb)** — Membership expired < 30 Tage, Guest Pass vom letzten Zyklus, Reciprocal-Claim ohne Match. Kontext: Ablaufdatum, Empfehlung, Renewal-Option.
128	
129	**✕ DENY (rot)** — Membership lapsed > 30 Tage, Token unbekannt, Duplicate Scan, Guest Pass consumed. Kontext: Grund, letzte Aktivität, Override-Option.
130	
131	---
132	
133	## 4 — Screens
134	
135	Der Prototyp hat drei Screens. Kein Login, kein Settings, kein Onboarding.
136	
137	### Screen A — Scan Station (Hauptinteraktion)
138	
139	Fullscreen-Fokus auf das Textfeld. Nach Token-Eingabe: sofortiger Wechsel zur Outcome-Anzeige mit Member/Guest-Kontext. Admit-Button prominent. Override-Flow als Secondary Action. Nach Admit: kurze Bestätigung, dann Reset für nächsten Scan.
140	
141	Elemente: Token-Textfeld mit Autofocus, Outcome-Card (grün/gelb/rot) mit Kontext-Feldern (Name, Tier, Membership-Status, verbleibende Guest Passes, Ablaufdatum), Admit-Button, Override-Dropdown, letzte 5 Scans als Mini-Log unten.
142	
143	### Screen B — Entry Dashboard
144	
145	Perspektive des Membership-Koordinators. Sieht, was vorher unsichtbar war.
146	
147	Elemente: Live Entry Count (heute), Guest Pass Count, Flags Count, Credential-Type-Breakdown (DM / Guest / Day Ticket), stündliche Traffic-Kurve für den aktuellen Tag, Flags-Queue (Duplicates, Overrides, Expired Admissions) mit Detail-Expand, Recent Entry Log mit Filter nach Credential Type und Outcome.
148	
149	### Screen C — Data Setup
150	
151	CSV-Upload für Member-Daten. Mapping-UI: User ordnet CSV-Spalten den Member-Feldern zu. Nach Import: Übersicht mit Anzahl importierter Records, Validierungsfehler. Zusätzlich: einfaches Formular zum manuellen Anlegen von Guest Passes (Token generieren, Referrer-Member auswählen).
152	
153	---
154	
155	## 5 — Credential Resolution Engine
156	
157	Die Engine ist das Herz des Prototyps. Sie muss production-nah sein, weil die gesamte Scan-Logik auf ihr aufbaut.
158	
159	```
160	// resolve(token)
161	
162	1. Token Lookup
163	   → Suche in members.dm_token
164	   → Suche in guests.guest_token
165	   → Kein Match → return { outcome: DENY, reason: "unknown_token" }
166	
167	2. Duplicate Check
168	   → SELECT FROM visits WHERE token = ? AND scanned_at > (now - window)
169	   → Match → flag "duplicate_scan", outcome abhängig von Severity-Config
170	
171	3. Entitlement Check (je nach credential_type)
172	   DM Pass:
173	     → membership_status == active? → ADMIT
174	     → expired < 30d? → REVIEW + renewal_hint
175	     → lapsed > 30d? → DENY
176	   Guest Pass:
177	     → guest.status == active? → ADMIT + set consumed on admit
178	     → guest.status == consumed? → DENY + "already_used"
179	     → guest.status == expired? → DENY
180	   Day Ticket:
181	     → valid_date == today? → ADMIT
182	     → else → DENY
183	
184	4. Context Assembly
185	   → Member: name, tier, expires_at, guest_passes_remaining
186	   → Guest: referrer_name, referrer_tier, wallet_added_at
187	   → Return: { outcome, credential_type, context, fraud_flags }
188	```
189	
190	---
191	
192	## 6 — Demo-Datensatz
193	
194	Der Prototyp kommt mit vorinstallierten Demo-Daten, die alle Outcome-Pfade abdecken.
195	
196	| Persona | Setup | Erwarteter Outcome |
197	|---|---|---|
198	| Maria Chen — Patron | Active, Tier: Patron, 4/6 Guest Passes übrig | 🟢 Admit, zeigt Tier + Guest Pass Count |
199	| Tom Rivera — Basic | Expired vor 12 Tagen | 🟡 Review, zeigt Ablaufdatum + Renewal-Hint |
200	| Sarah Kim — Family | Lapsed vor 4 Monaten | 🔴 Deny |
201	| Guest von Maria | Active Guest Pass, Phone captured | 🟢 Admit, markiert als consumed |
202	| Guest von Maria (2. Scan) | Gleicher Token, bereits consumed | 🔴 Deny + Flag "guest_reuse" |
203	| Tom Rivera (2. Scan in 5 min) | Override beim 1. Mal, jetzt Duplicate | 🔴 Deny + Flag "duplicate_scan" |
204	| Unknown Token | Token existiert nicht | 🔴 Deny, "unknown_token" |
205	
206	---
207	
208	## 7 — Explizit nicht enthalten
209	
210	Diese Entscheidungen sind bewusst. Jedes dieser Features wird erst gebaut, wenn der Kernflow validiert ist.
211	
212	| Feature | Warum nicht |
213	|---|---|
214	| Native Mobile App | Hardware-Scan ist simuliert. Mobile kommt erst, wenn die Datenlogik steht. |
215	| CRM Live-Sync | Jede CRM-Integration ist institution-spezifisch. CSV-Import reicht zum Beweisen. |
216	| Post-Visit SMS | SMS-Versand ist ein Twilio-Call. Im Prototyp: Button zeigt Preview + loggt Intent. |
217	| GPR+ Attribution Funnel | Braucht echte Guest-Conversions über Wochen. Wird als statischer Report mit Demo-Daten angedeutet. |
218	| Network Reporting | Pro-Tier Feature. Nicht relevant für Kern-Validierung. |
219	| Multi-Institution | Eine Institution reicht. Mandantenfähigkeit ist Architektur, nicht Prototyp. |
220	| Offline Mode | Erfordert Service Worker + Sync Queue. Irrelevant für Demo-Umgebung. |
221	| Pricing / Feature Flags | Business Logic, nicht Produkt-Validierung. |
222	
223	---
224	
225	## 8 — Der Prototyp ist erfolgreich, wenn …
226	
227	**01 — Scan → Outcome in <1 Sekunde.** Token eingeben, Ergebnis sehen. Die Latenz muss zeigen, dass Real-Time-Resolution machbar ist — auch wenn der „Scan" ein Textfeld ist.
228	
229	**02 — Kontext am Point of Action.** Der Staff-User sieht bei jedem Scan: Wer ist diese Person? Welches Tier? Wann läuft ab? Wie viele Guest Passes? Das ist der Unterschied zu „grün oder rot".
230	
231	**03 — Dashboard zeigt das Unsichtbare.** Der Koordinator öffnet das Dashboard und sieht zum ersten Mal: Wie viele Entries heute? Wie viele Guest Passes? Wie viele Overrides? Wie viele Flags? Die Antwort auf „Wie viel verlieren wir?" wird zum ersten Mal sichtbar.
232	
233	**04 — Datenmodell überlebt den Prototyp.** Die Tabellen, Relationen und Enum-Werte werden ohne Breaking Changes in Production übernommen.
234	
235	---
236	
237	*VAM Prototype Spec — Cuseum × BID Equity · April 2026*
238

## 1 — Prototype-Philosophie
